channel.title: Politics on 2 Hectic
channel.description: Recent content in Politics on 2 Hectic

item.title: Popularity and Subversion
item.description:
Recently, John Lydon (good thing he doesn’t go by Rotten anymore because he doesn’t deserve it) said that being conservative was the new punk because punk was always the opposite of what was the going concensus or ‘what was popular’ and sense ‘woke’ is popular, the punk thing to do is to oppose that. This is an astonishingly moronic assertion from a guy that I assumed got it. Didn’t he have to get it? Wasn’t that the point? And not just with the Sex Pistols, but on to PIL and beyond? This guy got it, right?


I’ve seen this before - in highschool. I came of age in the 80’s and 90’s and gravitated toward the punk subculture - whatever that may mean - once I got to highschool. Before the grunge scene broke, the alternative scene was incredibly heterogenous. You saw the same people at a They Might Be Giants show that you saw at a Cure show that you saw at a Rancid show - more or less. I have written extensively on this elsewhere. The alternative scene was perfectly by Perry Ferrell’s first Lollapalooza. That lineup defined the mentality of all the little subculture niches of alternative on one stage: it was about being genuine and not being merely a commodity. It was all DIY and it was fringe.


As a result, it was easy to differentiate ourselves from the Soc's: they listened to pop music and shopped at Dillards; we listened to community radio shows on certain days at certain times and we shopped at Crash-n-Burn. When all the events of the mid-nineties combined and the results became apparent - Ministry stickers on Jocks' pickup trucks, Doc Martins at Dillards, Green Day on the top 40 - we had to intellectualize what we were about without the popularity heuristic. And we did. Well, a lot of us did. A lot of us grew up but some of us committed to the bit. We abhorred the sell-out in principle, but we understood that it had become a monster that artists had no control over. Formerly, we had been our own gatekeepers. We made mixes for our square friends that introduced them to some cool shit but we chose those people and gave them a chance to have a little taste of what we were doing. Sometimes they wanted to shift gears and change their scene; most just thought it gave them a little street cred to know a few things about x, y, or z and that was fine too. Now there was no gate to be kept. Record companies want to sell records, they kind of have to. That doesn't justify the gross exploitation of artists but it does explain why we couldn't count on them to hold the line. And the bands have no control once they've signed off and if you're in a band and someone wants to make it so you can be a full time musician and not washing dishes at Perkins, you're gonna take it unless you really have some spine. Ani Difranco held her ground and Righteous Babe survives untouched. Most of the other labels that our DIY heroes started got subsumed just like the music festivals.

WHen I was young, I worked at a law firm in Clearwater, FL. They were a local business started by a handful of young lawyers in the late 70’s and did very well for themselves. Clearwater has a unique, if not well known, distinction in being host to the world headquarters of the Church of Scientology. To an outside it would seem weird to harbor a true disdain for Scientologists, even though their behavior has come undo scrutiny publicly in the last decade or so. However, if you live there, they are seen as a plague. And that was before they started acquiring huge swaths of property. That in and of itself wouldn’t be a big deal but their status as a Church allows them to not pay property taxes. This has decimated the downtown economy - not for the rich, of course, but for the middle class and the “walking around” folks. The own an estimated $600M in property in the downtown corridor and beyond - including several multi-family units for housing their members. That’s somewhere between $8-$12 million in tax revenue that the city is missing out on. Thats a whole other story but I bring it up because there was a long-standing directive at the firm that they weren’t going to do business with the Scientologists. In the 80’s, it hadn’t yet become so predatory a real estate model, but it was a point of contention socially but in the long run it was not an unreasonable policy and it wasn’t any kind of religious bigotry, it was just good business because old Clearwater wasn’t on board.

item.title: The story we need
item.description:
-

Times like this, we need stories. Personally, I like a nice heartwarming story about a kid and a horse. Or when kids get in over their heads and take on a little more than we think they’re capable of. Those are nice stories. They make me feel good. But feeling good doesn’t get you out of trouble. More often than not, it let’s you ignore the trouble until it’s knocking on your door. Even then, it can be tempting to hold your breath and mute the tv and duck under the covers - crouch and watch for shadows to stop moving under the door. Times like this we need a story about a person that’s just trying to keep his head down and be a worker among workers. You see this guy and he seems a little meek, but you’ve seen stories like this and your gut tells you that he’s just trying not to do something that’ll have consequences beyond his control. This guy has a past he isn’t all that comfortable with but, more than likely, afforded him a certain set of charater traits and skills that have a certain value in difficult circumstances. Liam Neeson’s warning to his daughter’s kidnapper i Taken captures this well - “a very particular set of skills. Skills I have acquired over a very long career. Skills that make me a nightmare for people like you. . .” But these anti-heroes are always former elite-something-or-others: a gunfighter, a space coyboy, a Navy SEAL, some kind of hacker that was at MIT when he was 8. Such a resume gives the writer some freedom to get the character out of seemingly-impossible situations - situations that are impossible for the layperson. But most of us are laypeople, or, more accurately, I am but I am also not totally helpless. That said, we are all helpless in many matters. We are powerless over the thoughts and feelings and the actions of others. We can react as we choose, and we can hope to

item.title: Nobody Move, Nobody Gets Hurt
item.description: This is everyone’s favorite part of the bank job. Our guys- we’re usually rooting for the robbers - are letting everyone know that they have no intention of committing wanton violence; they’re here for the bank’s money, not your money. You have no reason to be a hero, it will gain you nothing and we’ll be forced to maintain control by the means we have at our disposal. People will get hurt, it will be your fault and you’ll be right back in the same situation. Unfortunately, this is the least likely scenario that any of us will encounter where this sort of exchange takes place. I would love to watch a gang of tactically competent, smooth ass criminals walk out of a bank with untold riches. What is much more likely is that this is the tacit warning you will receive from an adversary, an abuser, or an oppressor. Behave. Don’t make me hurt you. Don’t force me to respond. Do not make waves. Do what you’re told or there will be consequences. How are we to receive this message? Do we trust that this person, these people, this institution has only our best interest in mind and wants to protect us from our poor understanding of things? Are we perhaps not privvy to certain knowledge that justifies the need for subserviance in the name of the greater good? The answer is always, without exception, “no.” This statement or its implication reveals fear - the fear that the recipient becomes aware of how precarious this apparent power imbalance is.

item.title: Woke
item.description: These terms seem to be used interchangeably quite often when they, in fact, have an inverse relationship. Liberty is your God-give right to self-actualize, to walk the Earth in search of adventure and enlightenment. If that’s not what you’re doing with it, I can’t help you. Freedom, on the other hand, is a gift from your fellow travelers that helps you maintain your liberty. Often this other is the state but in the long run and at it’s base it is a the gift we give each other whether it is part of a social contract to be a civilized, well-ordered people, whether it is in my self-interest to reciprocate such in principle, or whether I am simply indifferent to you. God gave you the tools to exploit the successes of those who reared and educated you and to recover from their mistakes. God gave you the ability to think critically and to conduct yourself in manner that will encourage your success whether it is as a member of that civilized, well-ordered group, whether you realize your success is contingent upon others’, or whether it’s by minding your own business. For those already hung up on the use of God so far, it is merely a shorthand that reflects the attitude of the author. The words above survive the replacement of God with whatever source of life or consciousness you ascribe to. Ulitmately, Liberty is innate to our being. It is Liberty that compels me to write this and it is freedom I tacitly request from you to allow me to express my interpretation of the world, the freedom I afford you to use it metaphorically to fit the way you interpret the world and it is something more interesting than either of those concepts that motivates us to understand one another - each in a language that makes most sense to us.
